---
title: "Step 06: Consider and Build in Process Uses if and as Appropriate"
description: "Detailed study notes for Step 06: Consider and Build in Process Uses if and as Appropriate in Essentials of Utilization-Focused Evaluation."
book_id: "essentials-utilization-focused-evaluation"
collection_id: "evaluation-texts"
note_type: "Step"
step_number: 6
source_pages: "163-190"
citation_key: "patton_2012_essentials_utilization_focused_evaluation"
evidence_ids:
  - "chunk:essentials-utilization-focused-evaluation:step06-step-6-consider-and-build-in-process-uses-if-and-as-appropriate:c0041"
  - "chunk:essentials-utilization-focused-evaluation:utilization-focused-evaluation-checklist-17-steps-to-evaluations-that-are-useful:c0012"
  - "chunk:essentials-utilization-focused-evaluation:interlude-steps-1-through-6-complex-dynamic-systems-interconnections-integrating:c0001"
source_status: "needs_review"
---

## Source

<div class="chapter-meta"><span>2012</span><span>MCP evaluation-texts</span><span>Status: needs_review</span><span>Step 6</span><span>Source pages 163-190</span></div>

This note summarises step 6, "Consider and Build in Process Uses if and as Appropriate," from Michael Quinn Patton's *Essentials of Utilization-Focused Evaluation* [@patton_2012_essentials_utilization_focused_evaluation]. It is a paraphrased study note based on source-linked MCP research-library retrieval records, not a substitute for the book section.

The retrieval trail is retained in the source records at the bottom of the page. The note paraphrases the section's argument and procedures, and it does not reproduce textbook tables, checklists, exhibits, graphics, or extended passages. When a checklist, exhibit, or graphic is central, the note describes its function and leaves the source artifact in the evidence record.

## Core Argument

The evaluation process itself can create learning, capacity, shared understanding, and changed behavior before findings are produced.

## Study Summary

Step 6 broadens use beyond final findings. Participating in evaluation can help users develop evaluative thinking, clarify assumptions, strengthen collaboration, or build capacity to use evidence.

Patton treats process use as intentional design work, not an incidental benefit. The evaluator should discuss process-use options with users, prioritize them, and design participation in ways that make the intended learning plausible.

The chapter also warns against dabbling. Process uses can compete with findings uses and with each other, so priorities must be explicit and manageable.

The interlude after Step 6 links findings uses and process uses as mutually interacting. What users learn through the process can reshape questions and methods, while intended findings uses should discipline how process participation is designed.

For close reading, track how this section uses process use, findings use, evaluative thinking, and primary intended users while moving through process-use options, capacity building, and learning and ownership. The notes below identify what the section asks the evaluator to notice, what use problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.

Within the wider book, this step belongs to the user-and-use sequence: it identifies who will use the evaluation, what they will use it for, and how the process itself can support use.

## Key Concepts

- **process use**: Learning, capacity building, shared understanding, or behavior change that occurs through participation in the evaluation process.
- **findings use**: Use of evaluation results, interpretations, judgments, or recommendations after data have been gathered.
- **evaluative thinking**: A disciplined habit of asking evidence-informed questions, making criteria explicit, and learning from results.
- **primary intended users**: Named people expected to use the evaluation and participate in major focus, design, interpretation, and use decisions.
- **adaptive use**: Ongoing adjustment of evaluation questions, methods, communication, or follow-up as users, evidence, and context change.

## Section Structure

- Process-use options.
- Capacity building.
- Learning and ownership.
- Priority setting.
- Findings-use integration.

## Practical Implications

- Design participation around intended learning, not generic involvement.
- Prioritize process uses just as explicitly as findings uses.
- Track whether process participation is strengthening or distracting from use.

## Concept Map

::: {.concept-map}
```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  Problem["Problem: What use problem does Step 6 help solve?"]
  Central["Concept: Consider and Build in Process Uses if and as Appropriate"]
  Concept1["Concept: process use"]
  Concept2["Concept: findings use"]
  Concept3["Concept: evaluative thinking"]
  Mechanism1["Mechanism: process-use options"]
  Mechanism2["Mechanism: capacity building"]
  Mechanism3["Mechanism: learning and ownership"]
  Implication1["Practice implication: Design participation around intended learning, not generic involvement."]
  Implication2["Practice implication: Prioritize process uses just as explicitly as findings uses."]
  Question["Open question: What would make this usable in a live evaluation?"]

  Problem --> Central
  Central --> Question
  Central --> Concept1
  Central --> Concept2
  Central --> Concept3
  Concept1 --> Mechanism1
  Concept2 --> Mechanism2
  Concept3 --> Mechanism3
  Mechanism1 --> Implication1
  Mechanism2 --> Implication2
  Implication1 --> Question

  classDef problem fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d;
  classDef concept fill:#e6f3f1,stroke:#115e59,color:#134e4a;
  classDef mechanism fill:#e8f1ff,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1e3a8a;
  classDef implication fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#831843;
  class Problem problem;
  class Central concept;
  class Concept1,Concept2,Concept3 concept;
  class Mechanism1,Mechanism2,Mechanism3 mechanism;
  class Implication1,Implication2,Question implication;
```
:::

## Connections

- Book overview: [Overview](index.qmd)
- Reading route: [Chapter Map](chapter-map.qmd)
- Concepts synthesis: [Core Concepts](concepts.qmd)
- Practice synthesis: [Practice Implications](practice-implications.qmd)
- Previous: [Step 5: Identify and Prioritize Primary Intended Uses by Determining Priority Purposes](step-05-identify-and-prioritize-primary-intended-uses.qmd)
- Next: [Step 7: Focus Priority Evaluation Questions](step-07-focus-priority-evaluation-questions.qmd)

## Study Prompts

1. What use problem does step 6 help solve?
2. Which primary intended users would need to participate for this guidance to work?
3. How would this section change scoping, methods, interpretation, reporting, or follow-up?
4. Where could this guidance be misused if treated as a fixed template?

## Source Records

<details class="source-chunks">
<summary>Source records</summary>
<ul class="source-chunk-list">
<li><code>chunk:essentials-utilization-focused-evaluation:step06-step-6-consider-and-build-in-process-uses-if-and-as-appropriate:c0041</code></li>
<li><code>chunk:essentials-utilization-focused-evaluation:utilization-focused-evaluation-checklist-17-steps-to-evaluations-that-are-useful:c0012</code></li>
<li><code>chunk:essentials-utilization-focused-evaluation:interlude-steps-1-through-6-complex-dynamic-systems-interconnections-integrating:c0001</code></li>
</ul>
</details>

## References

- Patton, M. Q. (2012). *Essentials of Utilization-Focused Evaluation*. SAGE Publications. Step 06: Consider and Build in Process Uses if and as Appropriate, pages 163-190.
- Saved retrieval metadata: `data/research-library/evidence/essentials-utilization-focused-evaluation/step-evidence-summary.json`.
